title: Rꜣ nw prt m hrw (125)
titleEn: Book of the Dead of Ani — Spell 125: opening declarations
source: Papyrus of Ani (British Museum EA 10470), Book of the Dead Spell 125. Reading layer drafted toward Ani's text. Hieroglyphs are set left-to-right for the reading view; the manuscript itself runs right-to-left (its true orientation belongs to the manuscript-image layer). Transliteration follows the Leiden Unified convention. Pronunciation convention: conventional Egyptological (langCode null — no TTS). PROVISIONAL DRAFT: the hieroglyphic forms and readings here are set from scholarly knowledge for pipeline testing and have NOT yet been verified sign-by-sign against Budge's 1913 facsimile of Ani — the sole-source requirement — which must happen before publication. Translations made fresh from the original grammar, not from any published translation.
about: These are the first lines of Spell 125, the 'Negative Confession', where the dead person declares innocence before the gods. This is a reading-and-language draft, not a finished edition: the hieroglyphs and their readings still need checking against the actual Ani papyrus, and a few signs are conventional best-guesses. Notes say so where it matters. The text is shown left-to-right for ease of reading; on the papyrus it runs right-to-left.

sentences.n: A rubric — written in red on the papyrus to mark the start of a recitation. The red carries meaning, so the reader keeps it. ḏd-mdw is literally 'saying words'.

The negation n heads every declaration — after a few lines you will read straight past it. The verb ꜥwꜣ is spelled out consonant by consonant, ꜥ–w–ꜣ. The seated man is the suffix pronoun 'I', shown here as its own unit (the split house rule).
The same frame as the line before: negation + verb + 'I'. smꜣ, 'to kill, slay', is spelled s–m–ꜣ. Watch how the little seated-man 'I' recurs at the end of every denial.

ir (lemma ı͗ri̯) 'do, make', here written with the eye. bw-ḏw, literally 'the bad thing' (bw 'thing, place' + ḏw 'bad'), is the ordinary word for 'evil' — a good early example of Egyptian building an abstract idea out of a concrete pair of words.

glossary.ḏd-mdw: ḏd-mdw — 'words to be spoken', a recitation rubric (ḏd 'say' + mdw 'words')
glossary.n: n — not; negates the following past-tense verb
glossary.·i: ·i — I, me, my (1st-person singular suffix pronoun; the seated-man sign)
glossary.ir: ir — to do, make (verb; lemma ı͗ri̯)
glossary.bw-ḏw: bw-ḏw — evil, wrongdoing (literally 'bad thing': bw 'thing' + ḏw 'bad')
